coredata.dc:title: Specific discrimination of zinc and manganese ions by label free dual emissive carbon dots by ratio-metric mode
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Haimin
Huang, Xinyu
Zhang, Fengxian
Luo, Xueting
Yu, Wenqian
Li, Cao
Jiang, Bingbing
Qiu, Zhenpeng
Xu, Ziqiang
coredata.dc:description: Due to the worldwide ecological and environmental issues induced by heavy metal pollution, including zinc and manganese, the ratio-metric discrimination of Zn2+ and Mn2+ based on CDs is urgently required. In this work, reduced CDs (re-CDs) with the intrinsic dual emissive peaks are obtained, and specific discrimination of Zn2+ and Mn2+ is realized by re-CDs with ratio-metric mode. With the addition of Zn2+, the fluorescent (FL) intensity at 650 nm increases obviously, while that at 680 nm progressively decreases. However, the presence of Mn2+ would induce the quenching of FL intensity at 680 nm while that at 650 nm remains constant. Then the Zn2+ and Mn2+ can be separately determined with the ratio of FL intensity at 650 nm to that at 680 mm (F 650/F 680). Under optimal conditions, the limit of detection (LOD) of Zn2+ is determined to be 9.09 nmol/L, and that for Mn2+ is estimated to be 0.93 nmol/L, which is much lower than previously reported work and standard level of Zn2+ and Mn2+ permitted in drinking water by WHO. Moreover, the specific recognition of Mn2+ and Zn2+ can be realized via the addition of different masking agents (ethylenediamine for Zn2+ and triethanolamine for Mn2+). Furthermore, our results reveal that the structural changes from –NH–CO to –NC–OH induced by Zn2+ contribute to the shift of FL peak from 680 to 650 nm while both static and dynamic quenching processes are involved in the detection of Mn2+. The ratio-metric probe was successfully applied to Zn2+ and Mn2+ determination in human serum samples and Sandy Lake water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Excellent sensitivity
Ratio-metric sensing
Zinc ions
Manganese ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of reduced state carbon dots (re-CDs)
Sensitive ratio-metric detection of Zn2+ or Mn2+
Selective detection of Zn2+ and Mn2+
Sensitive detection of Zn2+ or Mn2+ in normal human serum and lake water
Ratio-metric response of re-CDs towards Zn2+ and Mn2+
Determination of Zn2+ and Mn2+ in human serum samples and lake water

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22073025 , 21603067 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CDs (A) UV–vis absorption spectra and FL spectra of different CDs; (B) The FL decay curves of Raw CDs and ox-CDs excited by 375 nm with emission at 680 nm. (C) The HRTEM image of r-CDs. Scale Bar: 20 nm. Inset: size distribution of r-CDs. (D) The XRD patterns of different CDs, (E) The XPS survey spectrum of different CDs. (F) The FTIR spectra of raw CDs and r-CDs.

The feasibility of detection of Zn2+ and Mn2+. (A) The FL spectra of re-CDs incubated with different amounts of Zn2+. (B) The FL spectra of re-CDs incubated with different amounts of Mn2+. (C) The plots of F650/F680 relative to the concentration of Zn2+. (D) The plots of F650/F680 relative to the concentration of Mn2+.

(A)The UV–vis absorption spectra of re-CDs, re-CDs + Zn2+, and re-CDs + Mn2+. (B) The UV–vis absorption spectra of re-CDs in alkaline environment. (C) The FTIR spectra of the re-CDs before and after addition of Zn2+ and Mn2+. (D) The FL decay curves of re-CDs incubated with Zn2+. (E) The FL decay curves of re-CDs incubated with Mn2+. (F) The Zeta potential of different samples.

The FL spectra of re-CDs mixed with different concentration of Zn2+（A) and Mn2+ (D). The plots of F650/F680 versus the concentration of Zn2+ (B) and Mn2+ (E). The linear relationship between the F650/F680 and the concentration of Zn2+ (C) or Mn2+ (F). Error bars indicate standard deviations (n = 3).

The selectivity and anti-interference of the strategy for detection of Zn2+ and Mn2+. (A) The FL spectra of re-CDs in the presence of different metal ions (2.25 μmol/mL). (B) The influence of other metal ions on the F650/F680. (C) The influence of other small molecules on the F650/F680. (D) The anti-interference abilities of re-CDs on the detection of Zn2+ or Mn2+. Error bars indicate standard deviations (n = 3).
The scheme for the detection of Zn2+ and Mn2+ by specific binding of dual-emission re-CDs to Zn2+ and Mn2+ ions.
Recovery test of Zn2+, Mn2+in spiked human serum samples analyzed by the re-CDs-based ratio-metric FL nanoprobe (n = 5).
Recovery test of Zn2+, Mn2+ in spiked lake water analyzed by the r-CDs-based ratio-metric FL nanoprobe (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Specific discrimination of zinc and manganese ions by label free dual emissive carbon dots by ratio-metric mode

Hubei Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to the worldwide ecological and environmental issues induced by heavy metal pollution, including zinc and manganese, the ratio-metric discrimination of Zn2+ and Mn2+ based on CDs is urgently required. In this work, reduced CDs (re-CDs) with the intrinsic dual emissive peaks are obtained, and specific discrimination of Zn2+ and Mn2+ is realized by re-CDs with ratio-metric mode. With the addition of Zn2+, the fluorescent (FL) intensity at 650 nm increases obviously, while that at 680 nm progressively decreases. However, the presence of Mn2+ would induce the quenching of FL intensity at 680 nm while that at 650 nm remains constant. Then the Zn2+ and Mn2+ can be separately determined with the ratio of FL intensity at 650 nm to that at 680 mm (F 650/F 680). Under optimal conditions, the limit of detection (LOD) of Zn2+ is determined to be 9.09 nmol/L, and that for Mn2+ is estimated to be 0.93 nmol/L, which is much lower than previously reported work and standard level of Zn2+ and Mn2+ permitted in drinking water by WHO. Moreover, the specific recognition of Mn2+ and Zn2+ can be realized via the addition of different masking agents (ethylenediamine for Zn2+ and triethanolamine for Mn2+). Furthermore, our results reveal that the structural changes from –NH–CO to –NC–OH induced by Zn2+ contribute to the shift of FL peak from 680 to 650 nm while both static and dynamic quenching processes are involved in the detection of Mn2+. The ratio-metric probe was successfully applied to Zn2+ and Mn2+ determination in human serum samples and Sandy Lake water.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal pollution is one the most serious worldwide threatens to the ecology and environments, with the rapid development of mineral extraction and battery industries [1–3]. A large number of soils, lake-water and groundwater around the industrial and mining areas are contaminated with heavy metal [4–6]. Although the zinc in trace amounts is considered as essential element for biochemical process, zinc is still listed in the list of main contaminations proposed by the U.S. Environmental Protecting Agency, due to its potential toxic effects. In general, the zinc poisoning contributes to the desiccating muscles, disharmony and stomach ache [7]. Moreover, although manganese is another essential trace element in living body and has made great contributions to the development of national economy, serious environmental pollution and healthy issues have also resulted from the over-exposure to the manganese [8], such as affecting the nervous and respiratory systems and causing adverse neurological consequences [9]. Herein, highly sensitive fluorescent probes to analyze ions have rarely been reported for dual ions discrimination.

Although Zn2+ and Mn2+ can be effectively and accurately determined by several large-scale instruments, such as atomic absorption spectrometry [10] and inductive coupled plasma mass spectrometry [11], these techniques are associated with several limitations, such as requiring expensive instrumentation and complicated/time-consuming procedure. In contrast to the above strategies, fluorescent (FL) analysis of metal ions is superior in simplicity, high sensitivity and rapid response and is more suitable for real-time detection [12]. Recently, there are several works reporting the simultaneous detection of Mn2+ and Zn2+ with poor sensitivity by organic FL molecules [13,14]. The tedious synthetic routes of organic probe limit their application. Moreover, most of these FL probes rely on changes of a single peak, including FL enhancement or quenching [15,16]. However, these FL techniques are pretty sensitive to the concentration of the FL probe and intensity of excitation light, resulting in lower sensitivity and accuracy during detection [17]. In order to address these drawbacks, FL analysis based on nanomaterials ratio-metric strategy, which involves the introduction of another FL signal has gradually emerged as another promising direction [18]. The ratio-metric FL analysis with a built-in self-calibration function that detect analytes based on the ratio of FL intensities at the two peaks has been established to eliminate various external interference to realize accuracy and sensitive detection [19].

Hence, a series of strategies have been devoted to fabricate a ratio-metric FL system, which is mainly based on conjugation or embedding of fluorophores onto another FL matrix or non-FL substances [20–22]. Recently, Wang et al. realized the ratio-metric sensitive detection of Zn2+ and adenosine triphosphate based on the combination of carbon dots (CDs) with red emissions at 610 nm and quercetin which can emit bright green FL once coordinated with Zn2+ [23]. Yu et al. fabricated dual emissive Au nanocluster (AuNCs) based nano-complexs via conjugation of red emissive Au NCs and green emissive fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC), which can sensitive analyze Hg2+ [21]. Fan's group designed ratiometric FL probes for simultaneous detection of Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ by mixing the CDs and multicolor CdTe QDs without the introduction of recognition elements [20–22]. However, the tedious synthetic procedures and low stability limited their further application.

Reduced glutathione (GSH), Cysteine (Cys), glutamic acid (Glu), aspartic acid (Asp), glycine (Gly), histidine (His), and lysine (Lys) were purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd (China). The ascorbic acid (AA), Tris (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Tris), formamide (99%) ZnCl2, KCl, NaNO3, AlCl3, CoCl3, FeCl3, FeCl2·4H2O, CuCl2·H2O, Ni (NO3)2·6H2O, CrCl3, CdCl2, and MnCl2 were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. (China). All chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received. All aqueous solutions were prepared with deionized water.

FL spectroscopy was measured on a Hitachi FR-6000 spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded on a JEOL JEM-2100 (HR) electron microscope. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out on an ESCALAB 250Xi instrument (Thermo Scientific, USA) to study the element states. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was obtained by D8 Advance diffractometer equipped with a Cu Kα radiation source (Brook, Germany). The FL lifetime was measured on FLS1000 FL spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of reduced state carbon dots (re-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: In the present work, CDs were prepared using solvothermal treatment of glutathione solution in formamide according to previous work [27]. Then the synthesis of re-CDs with strong red luminescence by reducing the initial red luminescence CDs with sodium borohydride in an aqueous solution. In brief, 0.125 mol/L NaBH4 and CDs (10 mg) were mixed in 20 mL deionized water and the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 6 h. After the reaction, the pH value of the reactant solution was adjusted to neutral by HCl. Subsequently, the obtained solution is purified by dialyzing against ultrapure water through a dialysis membrane (MW = 1.0 KDa) for 24 h. Finally, the dark green solution re-CDs are obtained. The re-CDs can be stored for a long time at temperatures of 4 °C for future use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive ratio-metric detection of Zn2+ or Mn2+
ce:section.ce:para:
To determine the relative sensitivity of re-CDs (15 μg/mL) in detection of Zn2+ or Mn2+, the FL intensity of re-CDs incubated with different concentrations of metal ions. Namely, Zn2+ and Mn2+ (2 μL) solution with different concentrations was added into re-CDs solution (re-CDs (15 μL, 2 mg/mL), Tris-HAc buffer (1983 μL, 10 mmol/L, pH 7)) and then incubated at room temperature for 20 min. After that, the FL emission spectra were measured in the wavelength range from 600 to 800 nm under the excitation at 420 nm. The relative FL intensity F
650 /F
680 was denoted as the signal output value, in which F
650 and F
680 represent the FL intensities at 650 and 680 nm of the re-CDs in the presence of Zn2+ or Mn2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective detection of Zn2+ and Mn2+
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the selectivity of r-CDs towards different metal ions, 15 μL of r-CDs (2 mg/mL) were added to 90 μL of 0.05 mmol/L of ZnCl2, KCl, NaNO3, AlCl3, CoCl3, FeCl3, FeCl2·4H2O, CuCl2·H2O, Ni (NO3)2·6H2O, CrCl3, CdCl2, and MnCl2 in 1895 μL of Tris-HAc buffer (10 mmol/L, pH 7) solution, then their FL spectra were collected at room temperature. All experiments were performed three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive detection of Zn2+ or Mn2+ in normal human serum and lake water
ce:section.ce:para: The normal human serum is purchased from the Soarbio Co. (China). The lake water from Sandy Lake in Wuhan is selected as the real water for studying their feasibility in detecting real environmental samples. The normal human serum or filtrated lake water are then spiked with Zn2+ or Mn2+ to obtain solution with a different concentration of Zn2+(1.0, 1.2, 1.4, 1.6, 1.8 and 2.0 μmol/L) or Mn2+ (0.15, 0.2 and 0.25 μmol/L). Then the re-CDs were mixed with the above solution, and the content of Zn2+ or Mn2+ in the solutions was quantified by the FL intensity. All experiments were performed in triplicate.

Xiong's group has reported that the emission peaks of CDs are very sensitive to the surface functional groups [28]. Several works have reported that NaBH4 is an effective reductant to modulate the functional groups of CDs [29–31]. Fortunately, intrinsic dual emissive CDs (re-CDs) can be realized by reduction of raw CDs with NaBH4 (λ em = 650 nm and λ em = 680 nm) (Fig. 1 ). The concentration of NaBH4 and reaction time have been optimized accordingly (Fig. S1). In order to ensure higher overall FL intensity and the FL intensity at 650 nm to realize higher self-calibration ability, the raw CDs were reduced by 0.125 mol/L NaBH4 for 6 h. The UV–vis absorption spectra of raw CDs and re-CDs are very close to each other, except for the relative absorption of re-CDs between 350 and 450 nm becoming more intense, confirming the type of functional groups remaining constant after the reduction (Fig. 1A dotted line). The decay curves of both re-CDs and raw CDs can be well fitted with the second exponential decay model, and their average FL lifetime is very close to each other (Fig. 1B). In general, the emission of CDs mainly originates from the intrinsic emission and the surface defects emission [32]. Since the FL peak is focused on the red region, the intrinsic dual FL peak of re-CDs may have stemmed from the changes in surface functional groups.

Then the morphology of re-CDs is characterized by TEM accordingly. Large amounts of well-dispersed spherical particles were observed in the TEM images (Fig. 1C). Their average size is determined to be 2.8 nm, and the size distribution agrees well with the Gaussian distribution (inset of Fig. 1C). Compared with previous work [33], the size of re-CDs decreased obviously after the reduction, which may be caused by etching of the raw CDs by NaBH4. The XRD patterns of raw CDs and re-CDs reveal a typical diffraction peak centered around 27° (Fig. 1D), which is close to the characteristic peak of the graphitic (002) crystal plane at 26° [34]. This result confirms that the typical graphite structure of raw CDs remains unchanged after reduction by NaBH4.

Then we intend to explore the reason contributing to the additional FL peak at 650 nm. The XPS spectra of both CDs indicate the presence of C, N, O and S on the surface of CDs (Fig. 1E). The relative content of nitrogen decreases obviously while that of oxygen increases accordingly (Table S1). The high-resolution C1s and O1s XPS spectra of both CDs have been further analyzed. The C1s spectra of both CDs can be deconvoluted into four well-resolved peaks, ascribed to CC, C–N, CO and CO, respectively (Figs. S2A and S2B). The relative content of each surface component is listed in Table S2. After reduction by NaBH4, the content of C–O increases obviously while that of the C–N decreases dramatically accordingly. Meanwhile, the relative content of CC increased a little. Then the O1s spectra were fitted into two peaks (Figs. S2C and S2D). It is obvious that the content of CO (531.2 eV) decreases from 75.5% to 68.8% while the content of C–OH (532.8 eV) increases from 24.5% to 31.2% (Table S3). This result suggests that most carbonyl has been converted to hydroxyl after reduction by NaBH4. Meanwhile, the CC content increased slightly, and the nitrous C content decreased (Table S2). The changes in surface groups have been further clarified by the FTIR. As shown in Fig. 1F, the FTIR spectra of raw CDs and re-CDs exhibit several similar absorption bands. The broad peak at 3407 cm−1 is assigned to the O–H and N–H stretching vibrations, proving the presence of –OH and –NH2. The peak at 1673 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of CO while that at 1595 cm−1 can be ascribed to that of the CC/CN. Compared with raw CDs, the absorption band of νO-H/N–H increases obviously while the signal related to the νC
O decreases accordingly.

Based on the above results, the graphite structure of raw CDs remains constant after reduction and surface carbonyl has been converted to hydroxyl after reduction by NaBH4. Several works have attributed the shift of FL peak from 680 to 650 nm of raw CDs under alkaline conditions to the (de)protonation and tautomerism between the structures of –NH–CO and –NC–OH. Hence, the appearance of an additional peak at around 650 nm can be understood in this way.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratio-metric response of re-CDs towards Zn2+ and Mn2+
ce:section.ce:para:
Based on the superior intrinsic dual-emissive properties and abundant surface functional groups of re-CDs, the feasibility of ratio-metric sensing to detect Mn2+ and Zn2+ has been explored first. As shown in Fig. 2 A, with the addition of Zn2+, the FL intensity at 680 nm continuously decreased while that at 650 nm increases obviously, which indicates the possibility of ratio-metric sensing of Zn2+. Interestingly, in the presence of Mn2+, the FL intensity at 650 nm dramatically decreases while that at 680 nm almost remains constant (Fig. 2B), which is quite different from the result phenomena of Zn2+, implying the potential of differentiating of Zn2+ and Mn2+ by re-CDs in combination of FL intensity and spectra. The plots of the ratio of FL intensity of 680 nm to that of 650 nm (F
650 /F
680 ) relative to the concentration of metal ions (Zn2+ and Mn2+) are presented in Fig. 2C and D. Obviously, the F
650 /F
680 is positively related to the concentration of Zn2+ between 0 and 2.0 μmol/L and gradually slow down. Moreover, the F
650 /F
680 is positively correlated to the concentration of Mn2+within specific range. Hence, the analysis of the concentration of Mn2+ or Zn2+ can be realized in this way.

Since the response behavior of Zn2+ and Mn2+ are different, the molecular mechanism of interaction between CDs and metal ions needs to be elucidated. The interaction between Zn2+ and CDs is studied first. The gradual shift of the emissive peak from 680 to 650 nm may result from the coordinated interaction between Zn2+ and the surface group of re-CDs. In the presence of Zn2+, the UV–vis absorption of re-CDs changes dynamically, namely the absorption band at 675 nm disappears while another peak at 624 nm shifts to 640 nm. At the same time, the peak at 419 nm became more intense (Fig. 3 A), which further demonstrates the existence of strong interaction. Interestingly, we have found that the changes of absorption spectra in the presence of Zn2+ are quite similar to that under alkaline conditions (Fig. 3B). And several works attribute it to the (de)protonation and tautomerism between the structures of –NH–CO and –NC–OH [35,36]. These structure changes can also be confirmed by the FTIR spectra. With the addition of Zn2+, the absorption band at 1675 cm−1 disappeared while that at 1600 cm−1 divided into two peaks located at 1619 cm−1 and 1581 cm−1 appeared, which indicates the decrease of CO and increase of CN. Additionally, the absorption peak at 1305 cm−1 of C–N disappeared (Fig. 3C). These results further confirmed the structural changes between –NH–CO and –NC–OH, which is consistent with the trend of Zeta potential of re-CDs in the presence of Zn2+ (Fig. 3F). The addition of Zn2+ contributes to the decreases in average FL lifetime, mainly due to the ratio of fast component (2–3 ns) increases while the fraction of slow component (6–7 ns) decreases (Fig. 3D). We speculate that the structure of –NC–OH may have contributed to the fast decay routes.

Subsequently, the molecular mechanism between re-CDs and Mn2+ has been elucidated accordingly. Since the FL intensity at 680 nm can be gradually quenched by Mn2+ while that at 650 nm remains constant, the quenching process is analyzed by the Stern-Volmer (SV) plots. An upward curve is observed in SV plot (Fig. S3), concave towards y-axis, which indicates that both static and dynamic quenching processes are involved in the quenching behavior of Mn2+ towards FL of re-CDs [37]. With the addition of Mn2+, the absorption peaks at 398 and 419 nm shift to 424 and 447 nm while another two peaks at 628 and 675 nm shift to 641 and 702 nm. Moreover, a new broadband at around 545 nm appeared. Herein, Mn2+ would induce dramatic changes to the absorption spectrum of re-CDs (Fig. 3A), which is similar to Shi's report, demonstrating that the ground-state complex formation is involved in the FL quenching [38]. Due to the FL lifetime is the most reliable method to identify the quenching mechanism is static quenching or dynamic quenching, namely for static quenching, τ 0/τ = 1, while τ 0/τ < 1 is observed for dynamic quenching [39]. The average lifetime of re-CDs reduces from 5.72 to 4.30 ns (Fig. 3E), indicating that the dynamic quenching process drives the FL quenching of CDs by Mn2+, namely excited state re-CDs can be affected by the Mn2+, then relax to ground state without emission. Herein, the FL of re-CDs is quenched by Mn2+ via static and dynamic quenching. Since the lifetime of two decay components changes obviously, the molecular surface state changes obviously in the presence of Mn2+. Compared with raw CDs, the absorption band at 1595 cm−1 of νC
C/C
N disappeared. Another typical absorption at 1673 cm−1 of νC
O shifts to 1639 cm−1, which indicates the strong interactions between Mn2+ and carbonyl/heteroaromatic ring (Fig. 3C). Moreover, the surface charge of raw CDs is negatively charged, due to easy donate proton ability of surface covered –COOH and –OH groups. The negatively charged surface would favor the adsorption of positively charged Mn2+ (Fig. 3F).

Since the detection performance is related to the intrinsic FL features of re-CDs, the influence of concentration, pH and storage time on the FL properties of re-CDs were investigated. As depicted in Fig. S4A, apparent pH-dependent FL behavior is observed. The FL intensity of 680 nm increases obviously within pH 3 and 7. With the pH further increased from 8 to 12, the FL intensity at 680 nm gradually shifts to 650 nm (Fig. S4B), which is assumed to the deprotonation of surface groups of re-CDs under alkaline conditions and tautomerism between the structures of –NH–CO and –NC–OH on CDs surface [35]. In order to achieve better intrinsic FL features, the Tris-HAc buffer (pH = 7) was utilized in the following experiments. The FL feature of re-CDs is quite sensitive to their concentration. Both the FL intensity at 650 and 680 nm increase obviously within the range from 0 to 15 μg/mL, and then gradually decrease when the concentration further increases. The most significant difference in FL intensity at 680 and 650 nm is observed at 15 μg/mL, thus 15 μg/mL is selected in the following experiments (Figs. S4C and S4D). The FL properties of re-CDs are pretty stable, such as the ratio of F 680/F 650 almost remain constant within 12 h (Fig. S4E).

Then the influence of pH (from 3 to 7) on the detection of Zn2+ or Mn2+ has been explored. As for the re-CDs-Zn2+ system, the ratio of F
650 /F
680 increases obviously within pH from 4 to 6, and then gradually slows down and reaches the plateau within the pH from 6 to 8 (Fig. S5A). Hence, the optimal pH is set at 7 in the following experiments. Similar results have been observed in the re-CDs-Mn2+ system and the pH is also set at 7 in the following experiments (Fig. S5B). Subsequently, the influence of incubation time has been explored. After the Zn2+ is introduced, the ratio of F
650 /F
680 gradually increases and reaches a plateau after 5min (Fig. S5C). In order to realize the complete reaction between re-CDs and Zn2+ for the quantitative analysis, 10 min was chosen as the optimal reaction time in the following experiments. Similarly, in the presence of Mn2+, the plateau of F
650 /F
680 is reached within 15 min and 20 min is set for the detection of Mn2+ (Fig. S5D). Based on the above results, the detection of Zn2+ is performed in Tris-HAc (pH = 7) and 10 min incubation time while the detection of Mn2+ is performed in Tris-HAc (pH = 7) and 20 min incubation time.

Subsequently, the feasibility of re-CDs for analysis of metal ions is explored under optimal conditions. As depicted in Fig. 4 A, the FL intensity at 680 nm can be gradually quenched and that at 650 nm can be sensitized by the Zn2+ (from 0 to 2.25 μmol/L at an interval of 0.075 μmol/L). The F 680/F 650 relative to the concentration of Zn2+ is presented in Fig. 4B. With the addition of Zn2+, a sigmoid shape curve is observed and great linear relationship between the ratio of F 650/F 680 and concentration of Zn2+ is achieved within the range from 0.9 to 1.8 μmol/L (Fig. 4C). The linear regression equation is F 650/F 680 = 2.597 × [Zn2+]-1.022 with correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.996). Moreover, the limit of detection (LOD), based on 3σ/K, is determined to be 9.09 nmol/L (0.59 μg/mL), which is one order of magnitude lower than most existing FL detection platforms (Table S4) and especially the acceptable level for Zn2+ permitted in drinking water by WHO (5.0 mg/L). As for the detection of Mn2+, the FL intensity at 680 nm decreases obviously while that at 650 nm remains constant (Fig. 4D). The plots of F 650/F 680 relative to concentration of Mn2+ also displayed a sigmoid shape curve (Fig. 4E). Hence, the concentration of Mn2+ can be determined with a ratio-metric model within 0.15–0.3 μmol/L and the LOD is determined to be 0.932 nmol/L (Fig. 4F), which is 2 orders of magnitude lower than most previous work and especially the standard level of Mn2+ permitted in drinking water by WHO (0.1 mg/L) (Table S5).

The potential of the re-CDs for detection of metal ions highly relies on their superior selectivity and anti-interference ability. To investigate the selectivity of re-CDs towards different metal ions, the FL emission responses of re-CDs were examined separately by adding various metal ions (Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cd2+, K+, Li+, Na+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Mn2+ and Zn2+), respectively. Among these metal ions, only Zn2+ can induce the shift of FL peak of re-CDs from 680 to 650 nm. Completely quenching of the FL intensity at 680 nm and unchanged FL intensity at 650 nm can be only triggered by Mn2+ (Fig. 5 A). Therefore, compared with other metal ions, the Mn2+ and Zn2+ can be selectively and separately determined with ratio-metric mode.

In order to further confirm the anti-interference ability in the complex physiological environment of re-CDs during the detection of Mn2+ or Zn2+, the FL response of re-CDs in the presence of Zn2+ or Mn2+ mixed with other metal ions is studied (Fig. 5B). It is obvious that the ratio of F
650 /F
680 can be obviously enhanced, regardless of the presence of other metal ions. Moreover, compared with the blank group, the value of F
650 /F
680 is not affected by the presence of other metal ions (Fig. 5D). Although the co-existence of Mn2+ and Zn2+ still interferes with the detection of each other (Fig. S6), these influences can be eliminated via the addition of different masking agents. In the presence of ethylenediamine, the FL spectra of re-CDs incubated with Zn2+ and Mn2+ are similar to that incubated with sole Mn2+. Moreover, specific detection of Zn2+ can be realized by the presence of triethanolamine as a masking agent (Fig. S6). In addition, the anti-interference ability of re-CDs during the detection has been investigated. The co-existence of other small molecular substances, such as several types of amino acid, AA and GSH, with Mn2+ or Zn2+ does not result in any influence on the ratio of F
650 /F
680 (Fig. 5C). These results demonstrate the potential of re-CDs for the detection of Zn2+ and Mn2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Zn2+ and Mn2+ in human serum samples and lake water

ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the superior intrinsic dual emissive (λ em = 650 nm and λ em = 680 nm) properties of re-CDs with abundant surface functional groups were obtained by hydrothermal and post-reducing strategies. The extensive characterization was performed by HR-TRM, XPS, XRD and, FTIR spectroscopy. The graphite structure of raw CDs remains constant after reduction, and surface carbonyl has been converted to hydroxyl after reduction by NaBH4. The presence of Zn2+ can specifically enhance the FL at 650 nm remarkably while quenching the FL at 680 nm, which may have originated from the coordinated interaction between Zn2+ and the surface group of re-CDs. As for the presence of Mn2+, though the FL intensity at 680 nm declines obviously, the FL intensity at 650 nm nearly keeps constant, and may result from static and dynamic quenching. Based on the unique ratio-metric FL response, the re-CDs based nanoprobe successfully realized the highly sensitive and selective determination of Zn2+ and Mn2+, with a low detection limit of 9.09 nmol/L and 0.932 nmol/L, respectively, which are much lower than previous work and standard level of Zn2+ and Mn2+ permitted in drinking water by WHO. Of course, there is a great potential to make efforts of CDs in the field of clinical diagnosis, environmental monitoring, and cell imaging, which is also the direction of our future efforts.

Haimin Li: Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Xinyu Huang：Investigation, Methodology, Fengxian Zhang: Investigation, Xueting Luo: Investigation, Wenqian Yu：Investigation, Methodology, Cao Li: review & editing. Bingbing Jiang: Writing - review & editing. Zhenpeng Qiu：Methodology, review & editing Ziqiang Xu: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22073025, 21603067).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2023.124627.
